fuzzy_prd:
We need a renderer that turns a thread's captured call-stack snapshot into a readable traceback, the way a debugger or crash reporter would show it. The renderer handles two views: a pure Python listing and a merged native/Python listing that interleaves C and Python frames. For the pure Python mode: print each frame as 'File ..., line N, in <function>', with the relevant source line beneath it when available. Internal placeholder frames should be skipped in this mode. Arguments and locals print below the source line with a specific header format. The thread header summarizes runtime conditions in brackets — GIL state and garbage collection status. Each traceback ends with a blank line. For merged native mode: we need to reconcile the native C frames with the Python frames by matching interpreter evaluation frames to Python call entry points. If they don't match, fall back to the Python-only listing with an unmergeable notice. When they do match, weave C and Python frames together. Interpreter-internal plumbing frames get filtered out — same filtering approach as our profiler uses for noise reduction. Inlined calls (multiple Python frames under one evaluation frame) all render together. In merged mode, those placeholder frames that were skipped in pure mode DO show up. The source line display depends on line numbers: a line number of 0 means show the last line of the available source.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: the thread line should be exactly 'Traceback for thread <tid> [<conditions>] (most recent call last):'. The conditions inside the brackets are comma-joined in a fixed order, with the GIL state first and the collection state after that. The GIL part is one of Has the GIL / Waiting for the GIL / Dropping the GIL, and the collection part is Garbage collecting when it applies. If there are no conditions at all, leave it as [].

Also, for the merged native view, the interpreter evaluation frames we count against in the native stack are version-specific: on Python 2.7 it’s PyEval_EvalFrameEx, and on Python 3.x it’s _PyEval_EvalFrameDefault. Those are the symbols we use for the reconciliation counting when lining native frames up with Python call entry points.

And for the fallback case where we can’t actually do the merge because the native side doesn’t give us enough to work with, the notice text needs to be exactly '* - Unable to merge native stack due to insufficient native information - *'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with thread/native) -> stdout formatted Python/native stack report

trigger_keywords: which native frames are filtered during merge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Filtered during merge: evaluation frames (_PyEval_EvalFrameDefault / PyEval_EvalFrameEx), symbols starting with 'Py' (private runtime helpers), PyObject_Call, function_vectorcall, and other internal call-helper symbols.

trigger_keywords: what source line is shown when lineno is 0
how is line 0 handled in source display
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a frame's lineno is 0, the LAST line of the available source text is shown. The source text is split by newlines and the final element is used.

trigger_keywords: what escape format for non-printable chars in arguments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Control characters and non-printable bytes are escaped: ESC (\x1b), newline -> \n, other non-printable bytes -> \xNN. Values are rendered on a single line with escaping applied.

Full PyStack rendering surface: empty stack output, thread headers with names and spacing variants, Python frame lines, native-frame interleaving, arguments and locals sections, trailing blank-line rules, current/native flags, mismatch/fallback cases, and deterministic stdout formatting.

The adapter formats the supplied thread/native structure. Preserve exact header text, indentation, blank lines, frame ordering, argument/local rendering, native merge markers, and empty-stack output from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same filtering approach as our profiler uses for noise reduction
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The internal-frame filter during native/Python merge: drops interpreter evaluation frames and interpreter-internal plumbing symbols (Py-prefixed helpers, call dispatch shims, known internal helpers).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_1_python_only_listing.json.description: Render a thread's pure Python call stack (no native interleaving requested). The output starts with a header line naming the thread and its (possibly empty) status bracket, followed by one indented frame line per Python frame in call order (most recent call last), each naming the source file, the line number and the function, and ends with a trailing blank line. When native interleaving is not requested, any supplied native frames are ignored entirely.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
    (Python) File "file2.py", line 2, in function2
    (Python) File "file3.py", line 3, in function3

feature1_2_empty_stack.json.description: When a thread has no Python frames and native interleaving is not requested, the formatter emits a single line stating that the frame stack for that thread id is empty.

cases.expected_output: The frame stack for thread 1 is empty

feature1_3_inline_source.json.description: When a Python frame's source file is available, the formatter prints the relevant source line, stripped of surrounding whitespace, on its own indented line beneath the frame's location line. The selected source line is the one at the frame's line number. Column-range position information present on a frame does not change the plain-text output: the full source line is shown regardless.

python_frames.source:
x = "This is the line 1"
x = "This is the line 2"
x = "This is the line 3"
x = "This is the line 4"

x = "This is the line 1"
x = "This is the line 2"
x = "This is the line 3"
x = "This is the line 4"

x = "This is the line 1"
x = "This is the line 2"
x = "This is the line 3"
x = "This is the line 4"

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
        x = "This is the line 1"
    (Python) File "file2.py", line 2, in function2
        x = "This is the line 2"
    (Python) File "file3.py", line 3, in function3
        x = "This is the line 3"

feature1_4_arguments_and_locals.json.description: When a Python frame carries call arguments and/or local variables, the formatter renders them beneath the frame: an 'Arguments:' header followed by one 'name: value' line per argument (when any arguments exist), then a 'Locals:' header followed by one 'name: value' line per local (when any locals exist). Argument values are rendered with non-printable and control characters escaped into a single visible line; a header section is omitted entirely when it has no entries.

python_frames.source:
x = "This is the line 1" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 2" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 3" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 4" or (1+1)

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
        x = "This is the line 1" or (1+1)
      Arguments:
        the_argument: some_value
        the_second_argument: 42
      Locals:
        the_local: some_other_value
        the_second_local: 7

python_frames.source:
x = "This is the line 1" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 2" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 3" or (1+1)
x = "This is the line 4" or (1+1)

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
        x = "This is the line 1" or (1+1)
      Arguments:
        the_argument: \x1b[6;30;42m some_value\nwith\nnewlines'\x1b[0m

feature1_5_shim_frames_skipped.json.description: Internal placeholder ('shim') frames are not shown in a pure Python listing. Frames flagged as shim are skipped entirely, and only the genuine Python frames are rendered, each with its source line when available.

feature2_thread_status.json.description: The header's status bracket reflects the thread's runtime condition. Holding the global lock yields a 'Has the GIL' marker. A native stack that is waiting to acquire the lock yields 'Waiting for the GIL'; one that is releasing it yields 'Dropping the GIL'. A native stack inside garbage collection, or the collection flag set while holding the lock, yields 'Garbage collecting'. Multiple active conditions are joined with commas inside the brackets.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [Has the GIL] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1

Traceback for thread 1 [Has the GIL,Garbage collecting] (most recent call last):
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1

feature3_1_unable_to_merge.json.description: When native interleaving is requested but the native and Python stacks cannot be reconciled (the number of interpreter evaluation frames in the native stack does not match the number of entry-point Python frames), the formatter emits a notice that the native stack could not be merged, then falls back to listing the Python frames (if any) as in a plain listing.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 57005 [] (most recent call last):
* - Unable to merge native stack due to insufficient native information - *

feature3_2_native_only_listing.json.description: When native interleaving is requested for a thread that has native frames but no Python frames, and there are no interpreter evaluation frames to reconcile, the native stack is rendered directly: one indented line per native frame naming its file, line, function and originating library.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (C) File "native_file1.c", line 1, in native_function1 (library.so)
    (C) File "native_file2.c", line 2, in native_function2 (library.so)
    (C) File "native_file3.c", line 3, in native_function3 (library.so)
    (C) File "native_file4.c", line 4, in native_function4 (library.so)

feature3_3_merged_interleave.json.description: When the native and Python stacks reconcile, the formatter interleaves them: each interpreter evaluation frame in the native stack is replaced by the corresponding Python frame, while genuine native frames are shown as C lines. The result alternates native and Python frames so a reader sees the full mixed call stack in order. Native frames that merely re-enter the interpreter are collapsed so each Python call appears once.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (C) File "native_file1.c", line 1, in native_function1 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
    (C) File "native_file2.c", line 2, in native_function2 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file2.py", line 2, in function2
    (C) File "native_file3.c", line 3, in native_function3 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file3.py", line 3, in function3
    (C) File "native_file4.c", line 4, in native_function4 (library.so)

feature3_4_internal_frames_filtered.json.description: During interleaving, native frames that represent interpreter-internal plumbing — re-entrant evaluation entry points, private runtime helpers, generic call/vector-call dispatch shims, and a known set of internal call helpers — are dropped from the output, so only meaningful native frames and their merged Python frames remain.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (C) File "native_file1.c", line 1, in native_function1 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
    (C) File "native_file2.c", line 2, in native_function2 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file2.py", line 2, in function2
    (C) File "native_file3.c", line 3, in native_function3 (library.so)

feature3_5_inlined_frames.json.description: Consecutive Python frames that share a single interpreter evaluation frame (inlined calls) are all emitted together under that one merged position: after the entry frame, every following non-entry frame is rendered before moving on to the next native frame. This holds whether only some calls are inlined or the entire Python chain collapses under one evaluation frame.

cases.expected_output:
Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (C) File "native_file1.c", line 1, in native_function1 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file1.py", line 1, in function1
    (Python) File "file2.py", line 2, in function2
    (C) File "native_file2.c", line 2, in native_function2 (library.so)
    (Python) File "file3.py", line 3, in function3
    (Python) File "file4.py", line 4, in function4
    (Python) File "file5.py", line 5, in function5
    (C) File "native_file3.c", line 3, in native_function3 (library.so)

feature3_6_shim_frames_in_merge.json.description: Unlike the pure Python listing, when interleaving native and Python stacks the internal placeholder ('shim') frames that sit between real Python frames ARE rendered as part of the merged output, including their source line when available.

Traceback for thread 1 [] (most recent call last):
    (C) File "native_file1.c", line 1, in native_function1 (library.so)
